hero.title: McDonald's – Interaction Design Studio Project
hero.oneLiner: A Winter Quarter interaction design concept exploring how fast ordering can feel clearer, calmer, and easier to complete.

context.text: Quick-service ordering often compresses multiple decisions into a few hurried moments: what to choose, how to customize it, whether a deal is worth it, and what comes next. In this Northwestern EDI project, I explored how McDonald's ordering could reduce friction without removing choice. The focus was not a visual rebrand. It was the interaction layer itself: how the system guides decisions, recovers from mistakes, and keeps the user moving without turning the experience into noise.

bullets: Mapped the ordering journey to identify moments where speed and clarity routinely break down
Defined interaction principles for lowering cognitive load while preserving customization depth
Designed key states for browsing, configuring, reviewing, and confirming an order
Explored progressive disclosure patterns so the interface reveals detail when needed instead of all at once
Built prototype flows to test how people reacted to simplified next actions under time pressure
Used placeholder brand-aligned assets to communicate the interaction concept before final polish

decisions.decision: Progressive disclosure vs exposing every option immediately
decisions.why: Helped the main task stay light while still supporting deeper customization when people wanted it.
decisions.tradeoff: Added extra taps for power users who already knew exactly what they wanted.

decisions.decision: Clear recovery paths vs ultra-minimal confirmation
decisions.why: Made it easier to adjust mistakes without restarting the flow under time pressure.
decisions.tradeoff: Took slightly more interface space than a purely speed-optimized approach.

decisions.why: Kept the order flow legible so offers supported the experience instead of competing with it.
decisions.tradeoff: Reduced the visual dominance of promotional content.

worked: Framing the problem around interaction friction made the concept feel more grounded than a cosmetic redesign.
Prototype flows made it easier to discuss decision load and recovery behavior with specificity.
The work highlighted how small hierarchy changes can improve speed without making people feel rushed.
didnt: Without the final research packet, the current writeup still uses a broad framing rather than the exact tested segment language.

outcome.text: The result was a concept and prototype showing how fast-food ordering can become easier by improving interaction logic rather than simply adding more visual energy. It gave me a concrete case study in designing for speed through clarity.

reflection.text: This project reinforced that speed is usually a byproduct of legibility. When the next action is obvious, people move faster without feeling pushed.

skills.label: Interaction Design
skills.description: Designing how the system behaves, guides, and recovers.

skills.label: Information Hierarchy
skills.description: Making the next action easy to find under time pressure.

skills.label: Prototype Testing
skills.description: Using flows to evaluate clarity and decision load.

skills.label: UX Strategy
skills.description: Balancing speed, choice, and business constraints.
